exercises.title: Account for Where the Disk Actually Went
concepts: table versus index versus total size
exercises.problemHtml:
"The database is large" is not a diagnosis. Before adding disk, find out what is using it — and the answer is often indexes rather than rows, or one table nobody remembered. PostgreSQL exposes three different sizes per relation and they answer different questions: the heap alone, the indexes, and everything including out-of-line storage.

  • Print heap ... — pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('events')), the table's own pages.
  • Print indexes ... — pg_indexes_size('events').
  • Print total ... — pg_total_relation_size('events'), and satisfy yourself that it accounts for the other two.
  • Print ratio ... — indexes as a percentage of the heap, rounded to a whole number. On many production tables this exceeds 100.
  • Print biggest ... — the largest object in the database as name=size, from pg_class joined to pg_namespace, restricted to ordinary tables in the public schema.
  • Print db_size ... — the whole database, with pg_database_size(current_database()).
exercises.inputSpec: The events table: 50,000 rows of about 50 bytes of text each, with a primary key and one index on user_id.
exercises.outputSpec: The heap is about 4.5 MB, the indexes together about 1.5 MB, and the total is their sum at about 6 MB; indexes come to a third of the heap; events is the largest table; and the database is about 13 MB.
exercises.constraints: Report sizes through the pg_size_* functions rather than counting rows. Restrict the largest-object query to ordinary tables so indexes and system relations do not dominate it.

examplesJson.input: pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size('events'))
examplesJson.explanation: The heap: the pages holding the rows themselves.

examplesJson.input: pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size('events'))
examplesJson.explanation: Every index on the table added together — here a primary key and one secondary index.

examplesJson.input: indexes as a percentage of the heap
examplesJson.explanation: Two modest indexes already cost half of what the data costs; six indexes on a wide table routinely cost more than the rows.

hintsJson: pg_relation_size is the heap; pg_indexes_size is all indexes; pg_total_relation_size includes both plus TOAST.
pg_size_pretty turns bytes into human-readable units — keep the raw bytes for arithmetic.
relkind = 'r' restricts pg_class to ordinary tables.
Large text or bytea columns live in a separate TOAST relation that only the total size accounts for.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The three functions answer three questions and conflating them is the usual reason a capacity estimate is wrong. pg_relation_size is the heap — the pages holding rows. pg_indexes_size is every index on the table summed. pg_total_relation_size is both plus the TOAST relation, where PostgreSQL stores values too large for a page, typically long text, JSON documents and bytea. A table whose rows look small but whose total size is enormous is almost always a TOAST story, and only the total reveals it.

The index ratio is the number worth watching over time. Two ordinary indexes already cost a third of what the data costs here; a table carrying six indexes frequently spends more space on indexes than on rows, and every one of them is also a write cost on each insert. Rising storage that turns out to be indexes points at exercise 8 of the indexing module — unused and redundant indexes — rather than at buying disk.

The largest-object query is the one to keep. Sorted by total size and restricted to relkind = 'r', it answers "where did the space go" in a single statement; without that restriction the result is dominated by individual indexes and system catalogues, which is technically true and operationally useless.

One caveat worth knowing before acting on these numbers: they measure allocated space, not useful space. A table can be mostly empty and still large, because deleted rows leave reusable but unreturned space behind. Distinguishing "big because of data" from "big because of bloat" is exercise 4, and doing it before a maintenance window is what prevents a pointless VACUUM FULL.

exercises.title: See Who Is Connected and What They Are Doing
exercises.problemHtml:
When a database is in trouble the first question is always the same: what is running right now. pg_stat_activity has one row per server process, with its state, its current query and the timestamps needed to work out how long it has been that way.

The state to understand is idle in transaction: a session that opened a transaction, did some work and then stopped talking. It holds its locks and its snapshot indefinitely, blocking other writers and preventing VACUUM from cleaning up — a leaked transaction from an application that forgot to commit is one of the most common production incidents.

  • Print mine ... — your own backend's state and database, from the row where pid = pg_backend_pid().
  • Open a second session with dblink, start a transaction in it that touches events — take an ACCESS SHARE lock, since dblink_exec refuses statements that return rows — and leave it open.
  • Print idle_in_txn N — the number of sessions in state idle in transaction.
  • Print oldest ... — for that session, whether it has been open for less than a minute, as a boolean, using xact_start.
  • Close the second transaction, then print idle_after N to confirm it is gone.
  • Print connections N and max_conn ... — the current connection count and the max_connections setting, the ratio an alert should watch.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table, plus a second session opened through dblink during the exercise.
exercises.outputSpec: Your own session reports state active on this database; one session is idle in transaction while the dblink transaction is open and none afterwards; that session is under a minute old; and the connection count is well below max_connections.
exercises.constraints: Do not terminate any backend in this exercise. Identify the idle transaction from pg_stat_activity rather than from knowing you opened it.

examplesJson.input: SELECT state FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid()
examplesJson.explanation: Your own session is running the query that asks the question, so it is always active.

examplesJson.input: counting state = 'idle in transaction' while the dblink transaction is open
examplesJson.explanation: A transaction left open shows up here — with its locks and its snapshot still held.

examplesJson.input: the same count after the transaction is rolled back
examplesJson.explanation: Ending the transaction releases everything; the row returns to idle.

hintsJson: pg_backend_pid() identifies your own row in pg_stat_activity.
state values include active, idle, idle in transaction and idle in transaction (aborted).
dblink_exec refuses statements that return rows — use LOCK TABLE or an UPDATE to keep the other transaction busy.
xact_start is when the transaction began; query_start is when the current statement began.
now() - xact_start is the age of a transaction — the number an alert should threshold on.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
pg_stat_activity is the first place to look during an incident, and the state column carries most of the meaning. active means a statement is executing right now. idle means a connection is open with no transaction — harmless, though it still consumes a connection slot. idle in transaction is the dangerous one: no work is happening, yet the session holds every lock it has taken and keeps its snapshot alive, which prevents VACUUM from removing rows newer than that snapshot anywhere in the database.

That last consequence is the one that surprises people. A single forgotten transaction in an application connection pool can stop cleanup across unrelated tables, so bloat grows, plans degrade, and the eventual symptom looks nothing like the cause. The diagnosis is exactly the query written here, and the alert is on now() - xact_start exceeding a threshold — minutes, not hours.

Two timestamps are easy to confuse. xact_start is when the transaction began; query_start is when the current statement began. A long-running query and a long-running transaction are different problems: the first is usually a missing index, the second is usually application code holding a transaction open across a network call.

The connection count against max_connections is the other number an alert should carry. PostgreSQL reserves slots for superusers, so an application hitting the limit fails to connect while an administrator can still get in to look — which is deliberate, and the reason a pooler such as PgBouncer belongs in front of any application that opens connections per request. When a session genuinely must be stopped, pg_cancel_backend(pid) cancels its current query and pg_terminate_backend(pid) disconnects it; prefer the first, since it does not discard the connection.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[pg_stat_activity] --> B[state active means a statement is running]
  A --> C[state idle means a connection with no transaction]
  A --> D[state idle in transaction]
  D --> E[holds locks and a snapshot]
  E --> F[blocks writers and stops VACUUM cleaning up]
  F --> G[alert on now minus xact_start]

exercises.title: Watch Dead Rows Accumulate and VACUUM Clean Them
exercises.problemHtml:
PostgreSQL never updates a row in place. An UPDATE writes a new version and marks the old one dead; a DELETE only marks. Those dead versions stay until VACUUM reclaims them, which is why a table that receives updates grows even when its row count does not.

  • Print before ... — live and dead tuple counts for events from pg_stat_user_tables, as live/dead.
  • Update half the table: UPDATE events SET seen = true WHERE id % 2 = 0.
  • Print immediately ... — the same counts read straight away.
  • Call pg_stat_force_next_flush(), then print after_flush .... Compare it with the previous line and note what the first reading would have led you to conclude.
  • Run VACUUM events and print after_vacuum ....
  • Print autovac ... — the value of autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor and autovacuum_vacuum_threshold, joined by a comma, the two settings that decide when this happens by itself.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table with 50,000 live rows and no dead ones at the start.
exercises.outputSpec: The table starts with 50,000 live and 0 dead; immediately after updating half the rows the counters still read 0 dead because statistics have not been flushed; after the flush 25,000 dead rows appear (with the live estimate inflated to 100,000); VACUUM returns the dead count to 0; and autovacuum triggers at 20 percent of the table plus 50 rows by default.
exercises.constraints: Read the counts from pg_stat_user_tables. Do not use VACUUM FULL — that is the next exercise.

examplesJson.input: the counters read immediately after the UPDATE
examplesJson.explanation: Statistics are accumulated per backend and flushed asynchronously, so an immediate read reports the old numbers.

examplesJson.input: the same read after pg_stat_force_next_flush()
examplesJson.explanation: Half the rows now have a dead previous version each. The live count reads 100,000 rather than 50,000 because the counter is an estimate maintained from insert and update activity, not a COUNT — another reason to treat these as signals rather than facts.

examplesJson.input: after VACUUM events
examplesJson.explanation: The dead versions are gone and their space is reusable — though the file has not shrunk.

hintsJson: n_live_tup and n_dead_tup live in pg_stat_user_tables.
Statistics are flushed asynchronously — force it before reading, or the numbers lag.
VACUUM marks space reusable; it does not return it to the operating system.
Autovacuum fires at scale_factor times the table size plus threshold dead rows.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Dead tuples are the price of MVCC and the reason PostgreSQL needs VACUUM at all. Because an update writes a new row version rather than overwriting, concurrent readers keep seeing the version valid for their snapshot — no read ever blocks a write. The cost is that the old versions accumulate, and 25,000 of them appear here from a single statement that changed no row count.

The asynchronous statistics are worth meeting deliberately. Read immediately after the update, the counters still say zero dead rows, and an operator trusting that reading would conclude the table is healthy. Counters are accumulated in each backend and flushed on a timer, so monitoring must either force a flush or accept a small lag — the same trap as reading idx_scan in the indexing module, and worth remembering as a general property rather than a quirk of one view.

VACUUM returns the dead count to zero, and the important detail is what it does not do: the file stays the same size. The space is marked reusable so future inserts fill it, which is exactly right for a table with steady churn — repeatedly shrinking and regrowing a file would be pure waste. Space is only returned to the operating system when the free space happens to be at the end of the file, or by the rewrite that the next exercise covers.

In production this is normally automatic. Autovacuum wakes periodically and vacuums a table once its dead rows exceed autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor × live rows — by default 50 plus 20 percent. That default is reasonable for small tables and far too lazy for large ones: 20 percent of a hundred-million-row table is twenty million dead rows before cleanup begins. Lowering the scale factor per table with ALTER TABLE … SET (autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.02) is one of the most common and most valuable pieces of PostgreSQL tuning.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[UPDATE a row] --> B[new version written]
  A --> C[old version marked dead]
  C --> D[still visible to older snapshots]
  D --> E[VACUUM reclaims once no snapshot needs it]
  E --> F[space reusable but the file does not shrink]
  G[autovacuum threshold plus scale factor] --> E

exercises.title: Measure Bloat, Then Decide Whether a Rewrite Is Worth It
exercises.problemHtml:
A table can be twice the size its data needs and report a perfectly normal row count. pgstattuple opens the file and measures what is actually inside: live bytes, dead bytes and free space. That measurement is what tells you whether an expensive rewrite is justified — guessing from the size alone leads to maintenance windows that achieve nothing.

  • Print clean ... — the dead-tuple percentage and table size from pgstattuple('events'), as pct/size, rounding the percentage.
  • Delete a third of the rows (WHERE id % 3 = 0) and print after_delete ... in the same format.
  • Run plain VACUUM events, print after_vacuum ..., and note which of the two numbers changed.
  • Run VACUUM FULL events, print after_full ..., and note which changed this time.
  • Print lock_needed ... — the lock mode VACUUM FULL requires, taken from pg_locks while holding it is impractical here, so report the constant string AccessExclusiveLock and be sure you know why it matters.
  • Print rows N to confirm the surviving row count is unchanged by either operation.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table as the previous exercise left it — 50,000 rows and about 6.8 MB after the update churn.
exercises.outputSpec: The table starts at 0 percent dead and 6824 kB; deleting a third pushes the dead percentage to 20 with the size unchanged; a plain VACUUM returns it to 0 percent with the size still 6824 kB; VACUUM FULL rewrites the file down to 3032 kB; and 33,334 rows survive throughout.
exercises.constraints: Measure with pgstattuple rather than inferring bloat from pg_relation_size. Do not use VACUUM FULL before measuring.

examplesJson.input: pgstattuple('events') after deleting a third of the rows
examplesJson.explanation: The rows are gone logically; their bytes are still in the file.

examplesJson.input: after a plain VACUUM
examplesJson.explanation: VACUUM marks the space reusable — the dead percentage falls, the file does not shrink.

examplesJson.input: after VACUUM FULL
examplesJson.explanation: The table is rewritten without the free space, which is the only way the file gets smaller — at the cost of an exclusive lock.

hintsJson: pgstattuple reads the whole relation, so it is a diagnostic tool rather than something to run constantly.
Compare table_len before and after: plain VACUUM does not change it.
VACUUM FULL rewrites the table and needs an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for the whole rewrite.
pg_repack achieves the same result without the long exclusive lock, at the cost of extra disk.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The two VACUUM forms do genuinely different things and the measurements separate them cleanly. Plain VACUUM drives the dead percentage to zero while leaving table_len untouched: the space is marked free and will be reused by future inserts, but it still belongs to the file. VACUUM FULL rewrites the table into a new file without the free space, so the size finally drops.

The reason to prefer the first is in the lock. VACUUM FULL takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for the entire rewrite, which blocks every reader and writer — on a large table that is minutes to hours of total unavailability, and it needs enough free disk to hold a second copy while it runs. Plain VACUUM runs concurrently with normal traffic and is what should be happening automatically all the time.

So the operational rule is: measure first, and rewrite only when the free space will not be reused. A table with steady insert traffic reclaims its own free space and needs nothing; a table that shrank permanently — a purge of old data, a one-off cleanup — is holding space it will never use, and that is the case a rewrite is for. Running VACUUM FULL on a busy table with normal churn is the classic well-intentioned mistake: an outage that buys back space the table promptly re-allocates.

Two practical notes. pgstattuple reads the entire relation, so it is a diagnostic run deliberately, not a monitoring query on a timer; the approximate variant pgstattuple_approx exists for large tables. And when a rewrite genuinely is needed on a production table, pg_repack does the same job while holding the exclusive lock only briefly at the end — the standard answer when downtime is not acceptable.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[DELETE a third of the rows] --> B[dead space in the file]
  B --> C[plain VACUUM]
  C --> D[dead percent zero and file size unchanged]
  D --> E{will the space be reused}
  E -->|yes steady inserts| F[do nothing more]
  E -->|no one-off purge| G[VACUUM FULL rewrites the file]
  G --> H[smaller file but ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for the whole rewrite]

exercises.title: Find the Session That Is Blocking Everyone Else
exercises.problemHtml:
"The site is frozen" usually means one session holds a lock and everything else is queued behind it. Diagnosing that takes two questions: which locks exist, and who is waiting for whom. PostgreSQL answers the first with pg_locks and the second with pg_blocking_pids(), which walks the wait chain for you.

  • Open a second session with dblink and have it take an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on events inside an open transaction.
  • Print locks_held ... — the number of AccessExclusiveLock entries on events in pg_locks.
  • Set lock_timeout to 300 ms, then try to take a conflicting SHARE lock yourself. Catch the failure and print blocked ... with the SQLSTATE.
  • Print waiting ... — whether any row in pg_locks for events is ungranted, at the moment of checking. Predict the answer before running it, remembering that your own attempt has already given up by then.
  • Release the second session's transaction, then print after_release ... — the result of taking the same SHARE lock now, reported as ok.
  • Print timeout_setting ... — the current value of lock_timeout, then reset it.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table and a second session opened through dblink that holds an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock during part of the exercise.
exercises.outputSpec: One AccessExclusiveLock is held on the table; the conflicting lock attempt fails with SQLSTATE 55P03 after 300 ms rather than waiting indefinitely; the ungranted-lock check reads false, because the only waiter had already timed out before it ran; once the holding transaction ends the same lock is granted immediately; and lock_timeout reports 300.
exercises.constraints: Do not terminate the blocking backend. Recover by letting lock_timeout fire and by ending the other transaction normally.

examplesJson.input: a second session holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on events
examplesJson.explanation: pg_locks shows the mode and the relation, which is how you identify what is actually held.

examplesJson.input: attempting a SHARE lock with lock_timeout = 300ms
examplesJson.explanation: lock_not_available — the statement gave up rather than queueing behind the holder indefinitely.

examplesJson.input: the same attempt after the holder rolls back
examplesJson.explanation: Locks are released at end of transaction, not at end of statement.

hintsJson: pg_locks has relation, mode and granted; join to pg_class to get the table name.
pg_blocking_pids(pid) returns the sessions a given backend is waiting for.
lock_timeout aborts a statement that waits too long for a lock; statement_timeout limits total run time.
A lock is held until the transaction ends, which is why an idle transaction is so damaging.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Locks in PostgreSQL are held until the end of the transaction, not the end of the statement. That single rule explains most blocking incidents: a session that took a lock and then went idle keeps it, and every conflicting statement queues behind it — which is why exercise 2's idle in transaction and this exercise are really the same problem seen from two angles.

pg_locks shows what is held and what is waiting; the granted column is the difference. Joining it to pg_class turns object ids into table names, and joining to pg_stat_activity turns pids into queries — the combination is the standard "what is blocking what" query worth keeping in a runbook. pg_blocking_pids(pid) is the shortcut for the same question and follows chains, which matters because the session you can see waiting is often blocked by another waiter rather than by the original holder.

The ungranted-lock check reading false is worth pausing on: a waiting lock is only visible in pg_locks while something is waiting, and the timeout had already fired. Catching a blocking incident therefore means sampling continuously or, better, alerting on pg_blocking_pids from a monitoring job — a one-off query run after the fact shows nothing, which is why blocking incidents are so often described as having "disappeared on their own".

lock_timeout is the guardrail that turns an outage into an error. Without it, a statement waits indefinitely for a lock, and one blocked statement holding its own locks can grow a queue that consumes every connection. With it, the statement fails after the limit with SQLSTATE 55P03 — lock_not_available — and the application can retry or report. Note the distinction from statement_timeout, which limits how long a statement may run: they cover different failures and both belong in production settings.

The practical guidance for schema changes follows directly. An ALTER TABLE that needs ACCESS EXCLUSIVE will queue behind any open transaction on that table — and, worse, every subsequent query queues behind it, so a migration waiting on a lock can take an application down while doing nothing. The standard defence is to set a short lock_timeout before the migration and retry: fail fast and try again, rather than blocking the world while waiting.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[session 1 BEGIN then LOCK TABLE] --> B[holds AccessExclusiveLock until commit or rollback]
  C[session 2 wants a conflicting lock] --> D{lock_timeout set}
  D -->|no| E[waits indefinitely and queues others behind it]
  D -->|yes| F[fails with 55P03 after the limit]
  B --> G[transaction ends and the lock is released]
  G --> H[waiting session proceeds]

exercises.title: Put Guardrails on Runaway Statements and Transactions
exercises.problemHtml:
A database with no timeouts has no upper bound on how badly a single mistake can go. Three settings put that bound in place, and each covers a different failure: statement_timeout for a query that runs too long, lock_timeout for one that waits too long for a lock, and idle_in_transaction_session_timeout for a transaction that is opened and forgotten.

  • Set statement_timeout to 200 ms in a second session opened through dblink, run a one-second sleep there, and catch the error it propagates back. Print slow_query ... with the SQLSTATE. It has to be done remotely: a statement timeout cancels the whole statement, including any DO block around it, so EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS in your own session cannot catch it.
  • Print fast_query ... — ok — after running a query that finishes well inside the same limit, to show the guardrail does not affect normal work.
  • Set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout to 500 ms in a second session opened through dblink, have it open a transaction, wait 1 second, and then print leaked ...: whether that session is still in state idle in transaction.
  • Print defaults ... — the shipped defaults of all three settings joined by commas, from pg_settings using boot_val. All three are zero, meaning unlimited.
  • Print recommendation ... — the constant string set per role not globally, and make sure you can say why a nightly report and a web request need different limits.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table and a second session opened through dblink for the idle-transaction demonstration.
exercises.outputSpec: The slow query is cancelled with SQLSTATE 57014; a fast query under the same limit succeeds; the idle transaction in the second session is terminated by its timeout so it no longer appears; and all three settings ship as 0, meaning no limit at all.
exercises.constraints: Demonstrate each timeout by triggering it, not by describing it. Reset every setting you change in your own session.

examplesJson.input: a one-second sleep in a session whose statement_timeout is 200ms
examplesJson.explanation: query_canceled — the statement was stopped at the limit. It must be triggered in another session, because a cancellation cannot be caught by an exception handler wrapping it.

examplesJson.input: a query finishing in a few milliseconds under the same limit
examplesJson.explanation: A timeout is a ceiling, not a cost — normal work is unaffected.

examplesJson.input: the shipped defaults of the three timeouts
examplesJson.explanation: PostgreSQL ships with no limits at all, so every one of these is something you must decide to set.

hintsJson: statement_timeout counts execution time; lock_timeout counts time spent waiting for a lock.
WHEN OTHERS does not catch query_canceled — name the condition explicitly if you must handle it.
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout terminates the session, not just the transaction.
boot_val in pg_settings is the value the server ships with, before any configuration.
ALTER ROLE ... SET statement_timeout applies a limit to one login role only.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The three timeouts are not variations on one idea; they bound three separate ways a session can consume resources forever. statement_timeout stops a query that is running too long — a missing index, an accidental cross join, a report someone ran against production. lock_timeout stops one that is waiting too long for a lock, which is what keeps a migration from freezing an application while it queues. idle_in_transaction_session_timeout terminates a session that opened a transaction and stopped working, releasing its locks and its snapshot so VACUUM can proceed.

That all three ship as 0 — unlimited — is the fact to take away. A default PostgreSQL installation will happily let one query run for a week. Setting them is a decision every production database needs, and the values differ by workload, which is why they belong per role rather than in postgresql.conf: a web request that should never exceed a few seconds and a nightly ETL that legitimately runs for an hour cannot share a limit. ALTER ROLE web_app SET statement_timeout = '5s' expresses that precisely, and an application can still raise its own limit per session when it knows better.

One implementation detail is worth meeting directly, because it shapes how these can be tested: a statement timeout cannot be caught by a BEGIN … EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS block wrapping the slow statement. The cancellation applies to the whole statement, including the DO block itself, so the handler never runs. Even relayed from another session through dblink it still escapes a WHEN OTHERS handler, because PL/pgSQL deliberately excludes query_canceled from OTHERS — otherwise a stray handler could swallow an operator's cancel request and make a runaway query unkillable. Catching it requires naming the condition: EXCEPTION WHEN query_canceled THEN. The same property is what makes statement_timeout a reliable guardrail: application code cannot accidentally swallow it.

SQLSTATE 57014 is query_canceled, the same code a manual pg_cancel_backend produces — so application error handling that already treats cancellation as retryable works for timeouts too. It is worth distinguishing from 55P03 in the previous exercise: one means "you ran too long", the other means "you waited too long", and they call for different responses.

One caution about the idle-transaction timeout: it terminates the whole session, not merely the transaction, so the client sees a dropped connection. That is deliberate — the alternative would leave the client believing its transaction is still open — but it means the value should be generous enough not to punish a slow human at a psql prompt, and connection pools should be configured to reconnect cleanly.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[a session goes wrong] --> B{how}
  B -->|running too long| C[statement_timeout cancels with 57014]
  B -->|waiting for a lock| D[lock_timeout aborts with 55P03]
  B -->|transaction left open| E[idle_in_transaction_session_timeout ends the session]
  F[all three ship as 0 meaning unlimited] --> G[set them per role to match the workload]

exercises.title: Read the Cache Hit Ratio and the I/O It Implies
concepts: hit ratio as a ratio not a target
exercises.problemHtml:
PostgreSQL reads pages through a shared buffer cache. A page found there costs microseconds; one that must come from disk costs orders of magnitude more. The hit ratio — buffer hits divided by total reads — is the standard summary, and it is genuinely useful as long as you know what it does and does not mean.

  • Reset the statistics with pg_stat_reset() so the window starts now.
  • Run a query that reads the whole events table, then a second identical one. Force a statistics flush.
  • Print table_io ... — heap_blks_hit and heap_blks_read for events from pg_statio_user_tables, as hit/read.
  • Print hit_ratio ... — the percentage, rounded, guarding against division by zero.
  • Print db_ratio ... — the same ratio computed for the whole database from pg_stat_database.
  • Print index_io ... — idx_blks_hit and idx_blks_read for the same table, since index I/O is tracked separately and is often the more interesting number.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table after the earlier exercises, with statistics reset at the start of this one.
exercises.outputSpec: Both passes are served entirely from shared buffers on this warm test database — 758 hits and 0 reads — so both the table and database ratios read 100 percent; index I/O is reported separately and is zero here because the counts used a sequential scan.
exercises.constraints: Reset statistics first so the numbers describe this exercise. Force a flush before reading them, as in the earlier monitoring exercises.

examplesJson.input: heap_blks_hit and heap_blks_read after two full passes
examplesJson.explanation: The table was already resident from the earlier exercises, so every page was a hit — a ratio of 100 percent means the working set fits, not that the database is fast.

examplesJson.input: the hit ratio as a percentage
examplesJson.explanation: A small table on an idle server — the number to watch in production is how this changes over time, not its absolute value.

examplesJson.input: idx_blks_hit versus idx_blks_read
examplesJson.explanation: Index pages are read far more often than heap pages, so their ratio usually matters more.

hintsJson: pg_statio_user_tables separates heap, index and TOAST I/O per table.
A "hit" means the page was in shared buffers, not that it avoided the operating system cache.
Guard the division: a table nobody has read has zero of both counters.
Counters are cumulative since the last reset — always know the window before quoting a ratio.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The mechanism is simple and the interpretation is where people go wrong. A "hit" means the page was already in PostgreSQL's shared buffers; a "read" means it was not, and had to be fetched — though frequently from the operating system's own cache rather than from a physical disk, which is why a low PostgreSQL hit ratio does not automatically mean slow storage.

The ratio is a symptom, not a target. The widely repeated "keep it above 99 percent" is a heuristic for OLTP workloads whose working set should fit in memory; it is meaningless for a warehouse that legitimately scans tables larger than RAM, where a low ratio is expected and correct. Tuning shared_buffers upward to chase the number is a classic mistake — beyond roughly a quarter of system memory it usually helps less than leaving that memory to the operating system cache.

What the numbers are genuinely good for is change. A ratio that was 99 percent last month and is 80 percent today says the working set has outgrown the cache, or a new query is scanning something large, and that is worth investigating. Per-table I/O narrows it further: the table whose heap_blks_read exploded is the one to look at, and comparing it against index I/O tells you whether the reads are lookups or scans.

Two habits keep the measurement honest. Counters are cumulative since the last reset — quoting a ratio without knowing the window is meaningless, and stats_reset in pg_stat_database tells you when it started. And the flush before reading is the same asynchronous-statistics trap seen throughout this module; reading immediately after the queries would report numbers from before them.

exercises.title: Take a Logical Backup and Prove It Restores
concepts: a backup is not a backup until restored
verifying by checksum not by row count
exercises.problemHtml:
Every database has backups. Far fewer have restores, and the difference is discovered at the worst possible moment. This exercise performs the smallest complete version of the discipline: export, destroy, restore, and verify by comparing content rather than by trusting that a file exists.

  • Print original ... — the row count and a content checksum of events, as count/checksum. Build the checksum with md5(string_agg(...)) over the ordered rows so it depends on the data, not on physical order.
  • Export the table to /tmp/events.csv with COPY … TO … WITH (FORMAT csv).
  • Simulate the disaster: TRUNCATE events, and print after_disaster N.
  • Restore with COPY … FROM, then print restored ... in the same count/checksum format.
  • Print verified ... — whether the two checksums are equal. A row count alone would not have proved this.
  • Print note ... — the constant logical export is not point in time, and be sure you can explain what a physical backup with WAL archiving gives you that this does not.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table as left by the earlier exercises, exported to a CSV file inside the server container.
exercises.outputSpec: The original count and checksum are recorded; the truncate leaves zero rows; the restore returns the same count and the identical checksum; and the verification is true.
exercises.constraints: Verify with a content checksum, not only a row count. Use COPY rather than an INSERT ... SELECT into a second table — the point is an export that leaves the database.

examplesJson.input: md5 over the ordered rows before and after
examplesJson.explanation: Ordering the input makes the checksum depend on content alone, so it is a real equality test.

examplesJson.input: TRUNCATE events
examplesJson.explanation: The failure is simulated rather than described, which is what makes this a drill.

examplesJson.input: comparing only row counts
examplesJson.output: would pass even if every value were wrong
examplesJson.explanation: A count proves quantity, not content — which is why restores are verified by checksum.

hintsJson: COPY ... TO writes on the server, so the path is inside the server’s filesystem.
string_agg over an ORDER BY makes the checksum independent of physical row order.
TRUNCATE is instant and cannot be rolled back outside a transaction — exactly the disaster you want to simulate.
A logical export captures one moment; recovering to an arbitrary time needs a base backup plus WAL.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The discipline this exercise encodes is the one that matters: a backup is a hypothesis until a restore confirms it. Backups fail silently in ordinary ways — a cron job that has been erroring for months, a dump that excludes a schema, a file written to a disk that is itself gone — and every one of those is discovered during the incident unless restores are rehearsed.

Verifying by content rather than by row count is the second half of the discipline. A count proves quantity; a checksum over ordered rows proves the values. Ordering matters because physical row order changes after a restore, so an unordered aggregate would differ for identical data and produce false alarms.

What this drill does not give you is worth stating plainly. COPY and pg_dump are logical backups: they capture the state at one moment, they restore into a running server, and they are portable across versions and architectures. They cannot recover to an arbitrary point in time, and on a large database a logical restore is slow, because every row is inserted and every index rebuilt. The complement is a physical backup — pg_basebackup plus archived WAL — which restores a byte-level copy and can then replay the log to any moment, which is what "we lost the last four minutes" versus "we lost since midnight" comes down to.

In practice a production setup wants both: a physical base backup with continuous WAL archiving for recovery, and periodic logical dumps for portability and for restoring a single table without touching the rest. And both want the same thing this exercise did — a scheduled, automated restore into a scratch instance, with the result verified rather than assumed.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[export with COPY or pg_dump] --> B[logical backup one moment in time]
  B --> C[restore into a scratch database]
  C --> D[compare a content checksum]
  D --> E{identical}
  E -->|yes| F[the backup is proven]
  E -->|no| G[fix the process before you need it]
  H[physical base backup plus archived WAL] --> I[recovery to any point in time]

exercises.title: Change a Schema Under Load Without Freezing the Application
concepts: the queue behind a blocked DDL
exercises.problemHtml:
The dangerous part of a migration is rarely the work — it is the lock. A statement needing ACCESS EXCLUSIVE waits for every open transaction on the table, and while it waits every new query queues behind it. A migration that would have taken 50 milliseconds can therefore take an application down for as long as one forgotten transaction stays open.

  • Print cheap ... — ok — after adding a nullable column, which takes the exclusive lock only briefly and rewrites nothing.
  • Print concurrent ... — ok — after building an index with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, which does not block writers.
  • Demonstrate the hazard: open a transaction in a second session through dblink that reads events and stays open. With lock_timeout at 300 ms, attempt ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN blocked_col INT, catch the failure and print blocked ... with the SQLSTATE.
  • End the other transaction and retry the same statement, printing retry ... as ok.
  • Print validate_pattern ... — the two statements that add a check constraint without a long lock, as one string: ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT.
  • Print columns N — the number of columns on events at the end.
exercises.inputSpec: The events table, with a second session opened through dblink holding a read transaction during part of the exercise.
exercises.outputSpec: Adding a nullable column and building an index concurrently both succeed; with a reader transaction open the ALTER fails with SQLSTATE 55P03 rather than blocking indefinitely; retrying after the reader finishes succeeds; and the table ends with 6 columns.
exercises.constraints: Set lock_timeout before attempting DDL. Do not terminate the blocking session — let the timeout fire and retry.

examplesJson.input: ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN note TEXT
examplesJson.explanation: A nullable column with no default is a catalogue change: it takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, but only for an instant and with no table rewrite.

examplesJson.input: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
examplesJson.explanation: It scans the table twice and never blocks writers, at the cost of being slower and unable to run inside a transaction.

examplesJson.input: the same ALTER while a read transaction is open, with lock_timeout set
examplesJson.explanation: Failing fast is the point: without the timeout the migration would wait and queue every other query behind it.

hintsJson: A nullable column with no default is instant in modern PostgreSQL; a volatile default still rewrites the table.
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block.
Always set a short lock_timeout before DDL, and retry rather than waiting.
ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID takes a brief lock; VALIDATE CONSTRAINT checks existing rows without blocking writes.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Two facts make DDL dangerous under load, and neither is about how much work the statement does. First, most ALTER TABLE forms need an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, which conflicts with everything — including plain SELECTs. Second, PostgreSQL's lock queue is ordered: once the ALTER is waiting, every subsequent query waits behind it, even though the lock holder in front is only a reader. A migration blocked by one idle transaction therefore blocks the entire application, which is why "the deploy took the site down and the migration hadn't even started" is such a common story.

The defence is to fail fast. Setting a short lock_timeout before DDL turns an indefinite outage into a failed statement — SQLSTATE 55P03 — that a deploy script can retry a few seconds later. Retrying is almost always the right response, because the blocking transaction is usually short-lived; what must be avoided is waiting.

Choosing cheap forms matters as much. Adding a nullable column with no default is a catalogue change in modern PostgreSQL: instant, no rewrite. Adding one with a constant default is also cheap since version 11, but a volatile default still rewrites every row, and a type change usually does too — the difference between a migration that takes a millisecond and one that rewrites a hundred gigabytes while holding an exclusive lock.

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is the standard tool for indexes on live tables: it scans twice and never blocks writers. The costs are real — it is slower, it cannot run inside a transaction block, and a failure leaves an invalid index behind that must be dropped — but for a production table it is the only acceptable option. The same idea generalises through NOT VALID: adding a constraint that way takes only a brief lock, and VALIDATE CONSTRAINT then checks the existing rows while writes continue. Splitting a change into a fast metadata step and a slow concurrent step is the pattern behind almost every zero-downtime migration.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[open reader transaction] --> B[holds a lock on events]
  C[ALTER TABLE wants ACCESS EXCLUSIVE] --> D[waits behind the reader]
  D --> E[every new query queues behind the ALTER]
  E --> F[application appears frozen]
  G[lock_timeout set] --> H[ALTER fails fast with 55P03]
  H --> I[retry a moment later]
  J[CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY] --> K[no writer blocking at all]

exercises.title: Capstone: Write the Health Check You Would Be Paged By
concepts: pass or fail rather than numbers
checks that would have caught earlier exercises
exercises.problemHtml:
A dashboard of numbers requires a human to interpret it. A health check states a threshold and returns a verdict, which is what an alert or a deployment gate needs. Build one covering the failure modes this module demonstrated.

Produce a single result set with one row per check, ordered by name, with columns check_name, value and status where status is PASS or FAIL:

  • connections — connections as a percentage of max_connections; fail above 80.
  • idle_in_transaction — the count of sessions idle in transaction for more than 5 minutes; fail above 0.
  • long_running — the count of queries running longer than 5 minutes; fail above 0.
  • blocked_sessions — the count of sessions with a non-empty pg_blocking_pids; fail above 0.
  • dead_tuple_pct — the highest dead-tuple percentage among user tables with at least 1,000 live rows; fail above 20.
  • cache_hit_pct — the database-wide buffer hit ratio; fail below 90.
  • unused_indexes — indexes on user tables with zero scans, excluding unique and primary-key indexes; fail above 5.

Then print failures N — the number of checks with status FAIL — the single number a deployment gate would read.
exercises.inputSpec: The database as left by the earlier exercises in this module, on an otherwise idle server.
exercises.outputSpec: Seven rows in alphabetical order, each with its measured value and a verdict: blocked_sessions 0, cache_hit_pct 100, connections 6, dead_tuple_pct 0, idle_in_transaction 0, long_running 0 and unused_indexes 2 — all PASS, so the failure count is 0. Each threshold corresponds to a failure mode demonstrated earlier in the module.
exercises.constraints: Every check must be a threshold with a verdict, not a raw number. Exclude unique and primary-key indexes from the unused-index check, since they exist to enforce constraints rather than to be scanned.

examplesJson.input: the idle_in_transaction check
examplesJson.explanation: This is the check that would have caught the leaked transaction of exercise 2 before it blocked VACUUM.

examplesJson.input: the dead_tuple_pct check
examplesJson.output: the worst table’s percentage against a threshold of 20
examplesJson.explanation: It reproduces the autovacuum default as an alert, so a table that autovacuum is not keeping up with becomes visible.

examplesJson.input: the final tally
examplesJson.explanation: One number a deploy gate or an alert rule can act on, rather than seven graphs someone has to read.

hintsJson: Build each check as a SELECT producing a name, a value and a verdict, then UNION ALL them.
pg_blocking_pids(pid) returns an array — cardinality() greater than zero means the session is blocked.
Guard every division with NULLIF so an idle database does not produce a division-by-zero error.
Filter the dead-tuple check to tables with enough rows that the percentage is meaningful.

starterCodeJson.name: setup.sql
solution.sql

solutionCodeJson.name: solution.sql
solutionCodeJson.code:
WITH checks AS (
  SELECT 'connections' AS check_name,
         round(100.0 * (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity)
               / NULLIF((SELECT setting::int FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'max_connections'), 0)) AS value,
         80 AS limit_value, 'above' AS direction

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'idle_in_transaction',
         (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity
           WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND now() - xact_start > interval '5 minutes'),
         0, 'above'

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'long_running',
         (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity
           WHERE state = 'active' AND now() - query_start > interval '5 minutes'
             AND pid <> pg_backend_pid()),
         0, 'above'

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'blocked_sessions',
         (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE cardinality(pg_blocking_pids(pid)) > 0),
         0, 'above'

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'dead_tuple_pct',
         COALESCE((SELECT max(round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0)))
                   FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_live_tup >= 1000), 0),
         20, 'above'

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'cache_hit_pct',
         COALESCE((SELECT round(100.0 * blks_hit / NULLIF(blks_hit + blks_read, 0))
                   FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname = current_database()), 100),
         90, 'below'

  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'unused_indexes',
         (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s
           JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid
          WHERE s.idx_scan = 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary),
         5, 'above'
)
SELECT check_name,
       value,
       CASE WHEN direction = 'above' AND value > limit_value THEN 'FAIL'
            WHEN direction = 'below' AND value < limit_value THEN 'FAIL'
            ELSE 'PASS' END AS status
FROM checks
ORDER BY check_name;

WITH checks AS (
  SELECT round(100.0 * (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity)
         / NULLIF((SELECT setting::int FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'max_connections'), 0)) AS value,
         80 AS limit_value, 'above' AS direction
  UNION ALL SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND now() - xact_start > interval '5 minutes'), 0, 'above'
  UNION ALL SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active' AND now() - query_start > interval '5 minutes' AND pid <> pg_backend_pid()), 0, 'above'
  UNION ALL SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE cardinality(pg_blocking_pids(pid)) > 0), 0, 'above'
  UNION ALL SELECT COALESCE((SELECT max(round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0))) FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_live_tup >= 1000), 0), 20, 'above'
  UNION ALL SELECT COALESCE((SELECT round(100.0 * blks_hit / NULLIF(blks_hit + blks_read, 0)) FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname = current_database()), 100), 90, 'below'
  UNION ALL SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid WHERE s.idx_scan = 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary), 5, 'above'
)
SELECT count(*) FILTER (
  WHERE (direction = 'above' AND value > limit_value) OR (direction = 'below' AND value < limit_value)
) AS failures
FROM checks;

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The difference between monitoring and alerting is the threshold. A graph of dead tuples requires someone to look at it and know what normal is; dead_tuple_pct > 20 → FAIL encodes that knowledge once and acts on it forever. Every check here corresponds to a failure this module demonstrated, which is the right way to choose them — a check exists because something went wrong, or would have.

Read them as a set. idle_in_transaction catches the leaked transaction that holds locks and stops VACUUM. blocked_sessions catches the pile-up behind a blocking DDL. dead_tuple_pct catches autovacuum falling behind, using the same 20 percent that the default scale factor implies. connections catches the pool leak before the application cannot connect at all. Each is one query and one number, which is what makes them usable in a deployment gate, a cron job, or a Prometheus exporter without modification.

Two design decisions are worth defending. The unused-index check excludes unique and primary-key indexes deliberately: they exist to enforce constraints, and a zero scan count says nothing about whether they should be dropped — an alert that told an operator to drop a primary key would be worse than no alert. And every division is guarded with NULLIF, because an idle database really does have zero blocks read, and a health check that crashes on a quiet system is a health check nobody will keep.

The largest caveat is the measurement window. All of these counters are cumulative since the last pg_stat_reset() or server restart, so a cache hit ratio or an unused-index count taken minutes after a restart is meaningless. A production version should read stats_reset from pg_stat_database and skip the cumulative checks when the window is too short — the same discipline as the earlier exercises, one level up. What this check cannot see is also worth naming: replication lag, disk space, WAL accumulation and backup freshness live outside these views, and a real production check needs them too.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[raw metric] --> B[threshold]
  B --> C[PASS or FAIL verdict]
  C --> D[one failure count]
  D --> E[deploy gate or alert rule]
  F[idle in transaction] --> B
  G[blocked sessions] --> B
  H[dead tuple percent] --> B
  I[connections used] --> B
